tldr: Headlines across the corpus overwhelmingly frame Trump's Anthropic export ban and Europe's Palantir exit as a tech-sovereignty crisis — allies are breaking from US AI dependence. US domestic headlines add a distinctive security-military layer, naming Grok's use in Iranian strikes and a cybersecurity threat, angles that international outlets leave below the fold.

frames.sub_frame: tech sovereignty: allies break from US

evidence.quote: US export ban on Anthropic's AI models further strains
US curbs Anthropic AI access, raising global concerns
French spies drop AI giant Palantir over US overreliance
L'Europa comincia il distacco dall'Ai Usa
Trump's AI export strategy runs into Trump's export controls
the US government shut down Anthropic's latest Claude AI
fight with Anthropic is now a fight over cybersecurity
Elon Musk's AI tool Grok was used in strikes against Iran
Grok Is More Important Than Clean Air, DOJ Says
Trump's Justice Department Backs Elon Musk in Data Center
Musk backs 'Nuremberg trials' for Pakistani rape gangs

single_outlet_findings.finding: Both RT headlines cover Musk's European culture-war interventions (Nuremberg-trial call over Pakistani rape gangs; lawsuit against ZDF) — entirely absent from every other outlet. The Anthropic export ban and Palantir exit, the story's core, go uncovered.

'Elon Musk's AI tool Grok was used in strikes against Iran' is the only non-US headline to name Grok's military use explicitly, making the DOJ national-security rationale legible at headline level where European outlets leave it in the body text.

bottom_line: At headline level, this story is a tech-sovereignty narrative: six international outlets frame it as allies breaking from US AI overreliance, while US domestic headlines alone surface the military-AI dimension — Grok in Iranian strikes, cybersecurity at risk — that the rest of the world buries below the fold.
